mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-AMG GLA 35 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned GLA 35 (M260) engine typically costs £3,500–£12,000 supply only depending on condition and specification. Reconditioned M260 engines are available from UK suppliers with 12-month warranties. A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,200–£2,200 supply only, with total installed costs around £1,800–£2,800 for standard engines — AMG-specific M260 units command a premium. Fitting adds £900–£2,000 depending on workshop location. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-AMG GLA 35 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M260 is considered reliable in its early years of production. It benefits from modern materials, cooling and precise ECU management. However, it has known vulnerabilities: carbon build-up on intake valves is a common issue on direct-injection engines, CAMTRONIC system failures are rare but expensive to repair, and piston damage can occur if poor-quality fuel is used. A Chinese owner reported engine failure at 55,000 km requiring replacement of multiple components. Mercedes-Benz Canada has acknowledged M260 engine defects with an extended warranty program.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-AMG GLA 35?

acceptedAnswer.text: Carbon build-up on intake valves (rough idle, hesitation, loss of power), CAMTRONIC system failure (rough running, warning lights), piston damage from detonation (knocking, pinging), turbo actuator faults, oil and coolant leaks, and 48V mild-hybrid system issues. Using high-quality fuel and regular maintenance are essential for longevity.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-AMG GLA 35 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% Mercedes scrap threshold: if your GLA 35 is worth £25,000–£35,000 and a recon engine is £5,500–£7,000 (16–28%), it's worth it. If the vehicle is a tired £15,000 example, the same engine exceeds 35% — get a scrap valuation first. The GLA 35 is a desirable AMG SUV with strong enthusiast following — a good engine rebuild can add significant value if the rest of the car is in good condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-AMG GLA 35 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The GLA 35 uses the M260 — a 2.0-litre turbocharged inline-four producing 306 PS (225 kW) with 48V mild-hybrid assistance. It was fitted to the H247 GLA from 2020 onwards. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent carbon build-up on the intake valves?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — regular use of high-quality fuel (98 RON minimum) and occasional spirited driving can help reduce carbon build-up. Periodic walnut blasting of the intake valves is the most effective solution, recommended every 30,000–60,000 miles. Using a quality fuel system cleaner can also help, but walnut blasting is the only definitive solution for direct-injection engines.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market rates for AMG engines. Reconditioned M260 engines are available from UK suppliers with 12-month warranties. A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,200–£2,200 supply only, with total installed costs around £1,800–£2,800 for standard engines — AMG-specific M260 units command a significant premium. Main dealer new engines are substantially more expensive.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a GLA 35 engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The transverse M260 layout and complex 48V mild-hybrid system add labour time compared to non-hybrid engines.

problems.title: Carbon Build-up on Intake Valves (M260)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, hesitation on acceleration, reduced fuel economy, loss of power, misfires. As a direct-injection engine, the M260 does not wash the intake valves with fuel, allowing carbon deposits to accumulate over time.
problems.typicalMileage: 30,000–60,000 miles — accelerated by short journeys and poor-quality fuel.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, performance will continue to degrade.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: Carbon build-up is a well-known issue on direct-injection engines like the M260. The intake valves can become coated with carbon deposits, restricting airflow and causing rough running. Periodic walnut blasting of the intake valves is the most effective solution. Using high-quality fuel and occasional spirited driving can help reduce the rate of build-up.

problems.title: CAMTRONIC System Failure (M260)
problems.symptoms: Rough idling, engine warning light, reduced power, poor fuel economy, ticking or tapping noise from the engine. The CAMTRONIC system adjusts valve lift for improved efficiency and performance. Failures are rare but when they occur, repair is "very expensive".
problems.typicalMileage: 40,000–80,000 miles — can occur at relatively low mileage.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, performance and efficiency will suffer.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The CAMTRONIC system is a complex variable valve lift mechanism. If it fails, the engine may run roughly and trigger warning lights. Regular oil changes with the correct specification are essential for CAMTRONIC longevity. If you experience rough running or a check engine light, have the system diagnosed promptly.

problems.title: Piston Damage from Detonation (M260)
problems.symptoms: Engine knocking or pinging under load, loss of power, engine warning light, metallic noise from the engine. Use of poor-quality fuel can cause detonation (pre-ignition), leading to piston damage. In severe cases, piston rings can fail, causing oil consumption and compression loss.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — depends on fuel quality and driving conditions.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only if caught early; severe damage requires full engine replacement.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear knocking or pinging, stop driving and book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: The M260 is a high-performance engine with a 10.5:1 compression ratio. Using poor-quality fuel can cause detonation, which can damage pistons, rings and cylinder walls. Always use high-octane fuel (98 RON/93 AKI) as recommended by Mercedes-AMG. If you hear knocking or pinging under acceleration, stop driving immediately and have the engine inspected.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This GLA 35? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes using MB 229.51 specification oil
* Evidence of intake valve cleaning (walnut blasting) performed
* High-quality fuel used throughout ownership (98 RON minimum)
* No engine warning lights or rough running
avoidIf: * Rough idle or hesitation — carbon build-up or CAMTRONIC issues
* Knocking or pinging under load — potential piston damage from detonation
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — could indicate CAMTRONIC or turbo issues
* Poor service history — the M260 is unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The M260 is a modern, high-performance engine that offers impressive power in a compact package. It is considered reliable in its early years of production, but it has vulnerabilities: carbon build-up is a common issue on direct-injection engines, CAMTRONIC failures are rare but expensive, and piston damage can occur if poor-quality fuel is used. With the 70% Mercedes scrap threshold, a £5,000–£7,000 reconditioned engine on a GLA 35 valued at £25,000–£35,000 is 14–28% of the car's value — well worth it for a well-maintained example. On a sub-£15,000 example, think carefully. Mercedes-Benz Canada has acknowledged M260 engine defects with an extended warranty program, suggesting some inherent issues with the engine design.

cta.label: → Compare GLA 35 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-AMG GLA 35 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling, ticking or knocking on startup (CAMTRONIC or timing chain issues)
* Idle Quality — Any rough idle or hesitation? (carbon build-up or CAMTRONIC)
* Service History — Regular oil changes every 8,000–10,000 miles using MB 229.51 spec oil
* Fuel Quality — Has the car been run on high-octane fuel (98 RON minimum)?
* Intake Valve Cleaning — Has walnut blasting been performed? (recommended every 30,000–60,000 miles)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
